message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Respiratory epithelial cells are the primary target for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2). We investigated the 3D human airway tissue model to evaluate innate epithelial cell responses to SARS-CoV-2 infection. A SARS-CoV-2 clinical isolate productively infected the 3D-airway model with a time-dependent increase in viral load (VL) and concurrent upregulation of airway immunomodulatory factors (
                  <jats:italic>IL-6, ICAM-1</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>SCGB1A1</jats:italic>
                  ) and respiratory mucins (
                  <jats:italic>MUC5AC, MUC5B, MUC2</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>MUC4)</jats:italic>
                  , and differential modulation of select long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs i.e.,
                  <jats:italic>LASI, TOSL, NEAT1</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>MALAT1</jats:italic>
                  ). Next, we examined these immunomodulators in the COVID-19 patient nasopharyngeal swab samples collected from subjects with high- or low-VLs (∼100-fold difference). As compared to low-VL, high-VL patients had prominent mucoinflammatory signature with elevated expression of
                  <jats:italic>IL-6, ICAM-1, SCGB1A1, SPDEF, MUC5AC, MUC5B</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>MUC4</jats:italic>
                  . Interestingly,
                  <jats:italic>LASI, TOSL</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>NEAT1</jats:italic>
                  lncRNA expressions were also markedly elevated in high-VL patients with no change in
                  <jats:italic>MALAT1</jats:italic>
                  expression. In addition, dual-staining of
                  <jats:italic>LASI</jats:italic>
                  and SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid
                  <jats:italic>N1</jats:italic>
                  RNA showed predominantly nuclear/perinuclear localization at 24 hpi in 3D-airway model as well as in high-VL COVID-19 patient nasopharyngeal cells, which exhibited high MUC5AC immunopositivity. Collectively, these findings suggest SARS-CoV-2 induced lncRNAs may play a role in acute mucoinflammatory response observed in symptomatic COVID-19 patients.
                </jats:p>
title: Distinct Mucoinflammatory Phenotype and the Immunomodulatory Long Noncoding Transcripts Associated with SARS-CoV-2 Airway Infection
